ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Vietnamese-style crispy fried squid with batter coating; closest match is generic fried squid food product.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried squid generically; no specific Vietnamese-style battered preparation code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fried squid dish; not clinically coded as a distinct food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g (1 plate), which is a typical appetizer or side-dish portion of crispy fried squid as served in Vietnamese restaurants and food stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, fried (FDC ID 175168)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central entry for fried squid (mixed species, FDC ID 175168) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, cholesterol, and saturated fat are cross-referenced against this entry. The input record values (energy 275 kcal, protein 17.5 g, fat 19.0 g, carbohydrate 9.0 g, sodium 450 mg, cholesterol 225 mg, saturated fat 3.0 g) are broadly consistent with USDA fried squid data and are retained with adaptation notes. Micronutrient values (calcium 25 mg, iron 1.0 mg, vitamin C 2.5 mg) are plausible for fried squid and are retained. Trans fat 0.25 g is plausible from frying oil. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not reported in the input; set to -1. Potassium estimated at 280 mg/100g based on USDA squid data. Water content not reported; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese-style crispy fried squid. GI estimated at 55 based on the analogous food category of battered/fried seafood with wheat flour and cornstarch coating. The batter coating (wheat flour + cornstarch) is the primary glycaemic contributor; squid flesh itself has negligible carbohydrate. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI Database classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). GI category is therefore classified as 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 18.0) / 100 = 9.9, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 9.0 × 2 = 18.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 450 mg/100g (900 mg per standard 200 g serving), exceeding 39% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit in a single serving.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g, request dipping sauces (fish sauce, chilli sauce) on the side and use sparingly, and avoid adding extra salt, as the combined sodium from the batter seasoning and natural squid sodium already approaches the high-concern threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL 9.9 per 200 g serving) from the wheat flour and cornstarch batter coating, partially offset by high protein content (17.5 g/100g) which slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (100–150 g) of crispy fried squid occasionally, but should avoid sugar-containing dipping sauces, monitor total carbohydrate intake from the batter coating, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high cholesterol at 225 mg/100g (450 mg per 200 g serving — 150% of the 300 mg daily limit), combined with trans fat (0.25 g/100g) from deep-frying and high sodium (450 mg/100g) elevating cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat crispy fried squid as an occasional food only, limit portions to 80–100 g, avoid reused frying oil which increases trans fat content, and prioritise grilled or steamed squid preparations to substantially reduce cholesterol, trans fat, and sodium exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation and worsen joint symptoms in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried squid preparations; grilled or stir-fried squid provides the same protein and mineral benefits without the pro-inflammatory AGE burden generated by high-temperature frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fried Squid (Mực Chiên Giòn) is a popular Vietnamese appetizer and side dish consisting of fresh squid rings or whole baby squid coated in a seasoned batter of wheat flour and cornstarch, then deep-fried in hot vegetable oil until golden and crunchy. The dish is widely served in Vietnamese restaurants and street-food stalls across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The texture is characteristically crispy on the outside with tender, slightly chewy squid flesh inside. It is typically accompanied by a dipping sauce such as fish sauce with lime and chilli (nước chấm), sweet chilli sauce, or mayonnaise. Nutritionally, crispy fried squid is a high-protein food (17.5 g/100g) with moderate-to-high fat content (19.0 g/100g) from the frying oil, and a moderate carbohydrate contribution (9.0 g/100g) from the batter coating. Squid is naturally very high in cholesterol (225 mg/100g), which is a key clinical consideration for cardiovascular and lipid management. Sodium is elevated at 450 mg/100g from seasoning and natural squid sodium. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making this dish a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or chronic inflammatory conditions. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low category per University of Sydney classification) and the glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is approximately 9.9, driven by the batter rather than the squid itself. This dish is suitable for occasional consumption as part of a balanced diet but should be limited by individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or high cholesterol.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy fried squid?
Is mực chiên giòn high in cholesterol?
Is fried squid high in sodium?
What is the glycemic index of fried squid?
Is crispy fried squid suitable for hypertension?
How much protein is in fried squid?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried squid data (FDC ID 175168) and are broadly consistent with the input record values. The input record was AI-generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform source; values have been cross-validated against USDA data and retained where consistent. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for Vietnamese-style battered fried squid. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and several micronutrients are not available in the primary source for this specific preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Vietnamese-style battered fried squid not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175168) or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and proximate values are not reported in the primary adapted source for this specific fried squid preparation. Potassium estimated at 280 mg/100g based on USDA fried squid data. Input record contained duplicate portion entries (two identical '1 plate / 200g' entries); deduplicated to single entry. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a delivery/menu aggregator and is not an approved primary nutrient source; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central. The dish is classified as Vietnamese cuisine served in Singapore context; multilingual names include Vietnamese (mandatory per G6) and Chinese (contextually relevant for Singapore market). Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a Vietnamese dish, not a Singaporean dish; G6 Vietnamese rule requires only Vietnamese native-script name which is satisfied. GI category corrected to 'low' in iteration 3: glycemic_index 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI Database standard classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Previous iteration 2 incorrectly assigned 'medium'; this has been corrected.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; re-evaluated gi_category for GI 55 (incorrectly set to 'medium' — not yet corrected in that iteration).

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per University of Sydney GI Database standard classification. Updated gi_source notes to document this classification boundary. Updated llm_training description to reflect corrected GI category. This resolves the Q4 failure reported by the validator.
